title: A Tour Around Emacs

ast.value: reference public emacs elisp org-babel tutorial programming

ast: Welcome to Emacs! You are about to discover one of the most unusual and rewarding pieces of software ever written. Emacs is not just a text editor — it is a Lisp machine you live inside. Everything is programmable, everything is inspectable, and everything can be changed at runtime.
This tour uses org-babel, which lets you embed and
code blocks directly in org-mode documents. Place your cursor inside any
to evaluate it. The result appears right below.
Elisp (Emacs Lisp) is the language that defines Emacs itself. Every command, every keybinding, every behavior is written in it — and you can extend or override any of it.
function prints to the echo area (the little strip at the bottom of Emacs) and to the

ast.value: (message "Hello, Emacs! You are going to love it here.")

ast: Expected output: the string appears in the echo area and
Elisp uses prefix notation: the operator comes first.
In Emacs, everything lives in a
— a named chunk of text in memory. Files, shells, compilation output, help pages — all buffers.

ast.raw-value: Create a new buffer and write to it
title: Create a new buffer and write to it

ast: This creates a brand-new buffer called
, switches to it, and inserts a greeting.

ast.value:
(let ((buf (get-buffer-create "*My Playground*")))
  (with-current-buffer buf
    (erase-buffer)
    (insert "This buffer was created by elisp!\n")
    (insert "You can switch to it with C-x b *My Playground* RET\n"))
  (message "Buffer '%s' is ready — switch to it!" (buffer-name buf)))

ast: lets you operate on any buffer without switching your view to it. Very useful in scripts.
"Point" is the cursor position (a number: the character offset from the start of the buffer). "Mark" is a saved position you can jump back to. Together, point and mark define the active

ast.value:
(message "Before: point is at %d" (point))
(goto-char (point-min))
(message "After going to point-min: point is at %d" (point))

ast: This is one of the most important macros in Emacs. It runs code that moves point, then restores point to exactly where it was. Use it constantly.

ast.value:
(message "Point before: %d" (point))
(save-excursion
  (goto-char (point-min))
  (message "Inside save-excursion, point is: %d" (point)))
(message "Point restored to: %d" (point))

ast: Modifying buffer text is where Emacs gets really powerful. You can write code that writes code, reformats documents, or transforms data — all live, in the buffer.
This inserts a timestamp at the current cursor position.

ast.value:
(let ((buf (get-buffer-create "*Delete Demo*")))
  (with-current-buffer buf
    (erase-buffer)
    (insert "Hello, WORLD! Goodbye.")
    (goto-char 8)
    (delete-region 8 14)  ; removes "WORLD!"
    (message "Buffer now contains: '%s'" (buffer-string))))

ast: moves point to the end of the next match. Returns the end position, or signals an error if not found.

ast.value:
(with-current-buffer (get-buffer-create "*Search Demo*")
  (erase-buffer)
  (insert "The quick brown fox jumps over the lazy dog")
  (goto-char (point-min))
  (when (search-forward "fox" nil t)
    (message "Found 'fox' — point is now at %d" (point))))

(with-current-buffer (get-buffer-create "*Replace Demo*")
  (erase-buffer)
  (insert "cats are great. cats are clever. cats rule.")
  (goto-char (point-min))
  (while (search-forward "cats" nil t)
    (replace-match "dogs"))
  (message "Result: %s" (buffer-string)))

ast: opens a file (or creates a new buffer for a new file). This is the same function bound to

ast.value:
;; This opens your Emacs init file in a buffer
(find-file user-init-file)
(message "Opened: %s" user-init-file)

ast.raw-value: Read a file's contents into a string
title: Read a file's contents into a string

ast.value:
(let* ((path user-init-file)
       (contents (with-temp-buffer
                   (insert-file-contents path)
                   (buffer-string))))
  (message "First 100 chars of init file:\n%s"
           (substring contents 0 (min 100 (length contents)))))

ast.raw-value: Write a string to a file
title: Write a string to a file

ast.value:
(let ((tmpfile (make-temp-file "emacs-tour-")))
  (with-temp-file tmpfile
    (insert "Hello from elisp!\n")
    (insert (format "Written at: %s\n" (current-time-string))))
  (message "Wrote to: %s" tmpfile)
  ;; Read it back to confirm
  (message "Contents: %s"
           (with-temp-buffer
             (insert-file-contents tmpfile)
             (buffer-string))))

ast: Every keybinding in Emacs is just a mapping from a key sequence to a function (a
An "interactive" function is one that can be called with
or bound to a key. The

ast.value:
(defun my-say-hello ()
  "Say hello in the echo area. A friendly command."
  (interactive)
  (message "Hello from your very own command! M-x my-say-hello works!"))

(message "Command defined! Try M-x my-say-hello")

ast.raw-value: Bind it to a key globally
title: Bind it to a key globally

ast.value:
(global-set-key (kbd "C-c h") 'my-say-hello)
(message "Bound C-c h to my-say-hello — try pressing it!")

ast.raw-value: Bind a key in a specific mode
title: Bind a key in a specific mode

ast: Local bindings only apply in a specific major mode. This one binds a key only in

ast.value:
(defun my-text-mode-greeting ()
  "A greeting only available in text-mode."
  (interactive)
  (message "You're in text-mode! Great choice."))

(with-eval-after-load 'text-mode
  (define-key text-mode-map (kbd "C-c t") 'my-text-mode-greeting))

(message "Local keybinding registered for text-mode")

ast: Let's write something genuinely useful: a command that inserts the current date in a nice format.

ast.value:
(defun insert-today ()
  "Insert today's date at point in YYYY-MM-DD format.

Call interactively with M-x insert-today, or bind it to a key.
Example output: 2026-04-06"
  (interactive)
  (insert (format-time-string "%Y-%m-%d")))

;; Bind it for easy access
(global-set-key (kbd "C-c d") 'insert-today)

(message "Defined insert-today — press C-c d anywhere to insert today's date!")

ast: in any buffer. Today's date appears instantly.

ast.raw-value: A command with a prefix argument
title: A command with a prefix argument

ast: Prefix arguments let commands behave differently when called with

ast.value:
(defun my-greet (&optional arg)
  "Greet the user. With C-u prefix, be extra enthusiastic."
  (interactive "P")
  (if arg
      (message "HELLO!!! EMACS IS THE GREATEST EDITOR IN THE UNIVERSE!!!")
    (message "Hello! Welcome to Emacs.")))

(message "Try M-x my-greet, then try C-u M-x my-greet")

ast: Elisp is a Lisp — lists are fundamental. Everything is either an atom or a list. Code itself is made of lists.
builds a new list by prepending an element.

ast.value:
(let ((my-list '(1 2 3 4 5)))
  (list
   (car my-list)    ; first element: 1
   (cdr my-list)    ; rest: (2 3 4 5)
   (cadr my-list)   ; second element: 2
   (cons 0 my-list) ; prepend 0: (0 1 2 3 4 5)
   ))
(append '(a b c) '(d e f) '(g h i))

;; Get all buffer names containing "*", sorted alphabetically
(sort
 (cl-remove-if-not
  (lambda (name) (string-match-p "\\*" name))
  (mapcar #'buffer-name (buffer-list)))
 #'string<)

ast: Org-mode exposes a rich API for navigating and modifying org documents programmatically.

ast.value:
;; This works when you're in an org-mode buffer
(when (derived-mode-p 'org-mode)
  (org-back-to-heading t)
  (message "Heading: %s" (org-get-heading t t t t)))

ast.raw-value: Set a property on the current heading
title: Set a property on the current heading

ast.value:
(when (derived-mode-p 'org-mode)
  (org-insert-heading-after-current)
  (insert "New Heading from Elisp")
  (message "Inserted a new heading!"))

ast.raw-value: Collect all headings in the current buffer
title: Collect all headings in the current buffer

ast: Hooks are lists of functions that Emacs runs at specific moments — when entering a mode, saving a file, opening a buffer. They are the primary extension point for customizing behavior.

ast.value:
(defun my-text-mode-setup ()
  "Run when entering text-mode."
  (visual-line-mode 1)  ; wrap long lines visually
  (message "text-mode hook fired — visual-line-mode enabled"))

(add-hook 'text-mode-hook #'my-text-mode-setup)
(message "Hook added to text-mode-hook")

ast.raw-value: A before-save hook that timestamps your file
title: A before-save hook that timestamps your file

ast.value:
(defun my-update-timestamp ()
  "Update a LAST_MODIFIED timestamp at the top of the file."
  ;; Only do this in files that have the marker
  (when (and buffer-file-name
             (save-excursion
               (goto-char (point-min))
               (search-forward "#+LAST_MODIFIED:" nil t)))
    (save-excursion
      (goto-char (point-min))
      (search-forward "#+LAST_MODIFIED:")
      (delete-region (point) (line-end-position))
      (insert (format " %s" (format-time-string "%Y-%m-%d %H:%M"))))))

(add-hook 'before-save-hook #'my-update-timestamp)
(message "Timestamp hook installed")

ast: Most Emacs settings are controlled by variables. You can inspect and change them at runtime.

ast.value:
(message "fill-column is: %d" fill-column)
(message "tab-width is: %d" tab-width)
(message "user-full-name is: %s" user-full-name)

ast: This opens the help buffer for any variable. Great for exploration.

ast.raw-value: setq-default — set the default for buffer-local variables
title: setq-default — set the default for buffer-local variables

ast: — each buffer has its own value.

ast.value:
(setq-default fill-column 80)
(setq-default indent-tabs-mode nil)  ; spaces, not tabs
(message "Defaults updated")

ast: This is the "official" way to set variables defined with
. It runs any associated setter hooks.

ast.value:
(customize-set-variable 'show-trailing-whitespace t)
(message "Trailing whitespace will now be highlighted")

ast: Emacs has a built-in package manager and access to MELPA, the community package archive. There are thousands of packages.

ast.value:
(package-initialize)
(message "Packages initialized. M-x list-packages to browse everything.")

;; Check if a package is installed before trying to install it
(unless (package-installed-p 'which-key)
  (package-refresh-contents)
  (package-install 'which-key))
(message "which-key is available")

ast: is the standard way to configure packages in your init file. It's lazy, declarative, and keeps things organized.

ast.value:
;; This is what use-package declarations look like:
;; (use-package which-key
;;   :ensure t          ; install if not present
;;   :defer t           ; load lazily
;;   :diminish          ; hide from mode line
;;   :config
;;   (which-key-mode 1) ; enable after loading
;;   :bind
;;   ("C-h k" . which-key-show-top-level))

(message "use-package is the recommended way to configure packages")

ast: is how you introduce local variables in Elisp. It's not a macro in the traditional sense, but it's the foundational binding construct — and
allows each binding to reference the previous ones.
All bindings are evaluated before any of them are available.
Each binding can reference the ones that came before it.
You can record a keyboard macro with
, perform actions, then end recording with

ast.value:
;; Define a named macro programmatically
(fset 'my-insert-separator
      (lambda ()
        (interactive)
        (insert (make-string 60 ?-) "\n")))

(message "Try M-x my-insert-separator to insert a separator line")

(dolist (val '(1 "hello" (1 2 3) t nil))
  (message "%s is a %s"
           val
           (pcase val
             ((pred numberp) "number")
             ((pred stringp) "string")
             ((pred listp)   "list")
             ('t             "t (true)")
             ('nil           "nil (false)")
             (_              "something else"))))

ast: Emacs has its own regex dialect. It's powerful but uses heavy backslash escaping. In elisp strings, you need to double the backslashes.
Returns the position of the match, or nil if no match. Side effect: populates match data for use with

ast.value:
(let ((str "Email: user@example.com"))
  (when (string-match "\\([a-z]+\\)@\\([a-z.]+\\)" str)
    (message "Full match: %s" (match-string 0 str))
    (message "User: %s" (match-string 1 str))
    (message "Domain: %s" (match-string 2 str))))

ast: Use this when you only need to know

ast.value:
(let ((str "foo123 bar456 baz789")
      (pos 0)
      matches)
  (while (string-match "[0-9]+" str pos)
    (push (match-string 0 str) matches)
    (setq pos (match-end 0)))
  (message "Numbers found: %s" (nreverse matches)))

ast.raw-value: 15. A Mini Project: The Smart Daily Note Inserter
title: 15. A Mini Project: The Smart Daily Note Inserter

ast: Let's bring it all together. This mini-project defines a command that:
Counts words in the current buffer
Inserts a nicely formatted daily note entry at point

ast.value:
(defun my-count-words-in-buffer ()
  "Return the word count of the current buffer."
  (save-excursion
    (goto-char (point-min))
    (let ((count 0))
      (while (forward-word 1)
        (setq count (1+ count)))
      count)))

(defun my-insert-daily-note (topic)
  "Insert a formatted daily note entry at point.

Prompts for TOPIC. Includes date, time, buffer word count,
and a ready-to-fill template."
  (interactive "sTopic: ")
  (let* ((date (format-time-string "%Y-%m-%d"))
         (time (format-time-string "%H:%M"))
         (words (my-count-words-in-buffer))
         (entry (format
                 "\n** [%s %s] %s\n   :PROPERTIES:\n   :WORDS_AT_ENTRY: %d\n   :END:\n\n   "
                 date time topic words)))
    (insert entry)
    (message "Daily note inserted for: %s (%d words in buffer)" topic words)))

(global-set-key (kbd "C-c n") 'my-insert-daily-note)
(message "Ready! Press C-c n to insert a daily note, or M-x my-insert-daily-note")

(defun my-count-words-region (start end)
  "Count and report words in the active region."
  (interactive "r")
  (let ((count (how-many "\\w+" start end)))
    (message "Region contains %d words" count)))

(global-set-key (kbd "C-c w") 'my-count-words-region)
(message "C-c w will count words in the selected region")

ast.raw-value: Bonus: A function that generates a project summary
title: Bonus: A function that generates a project summary

ast.value:
(defun my-buffer-summary ()
  "Print a summary of the current buffer."
  (interactive)
  (let* ((name (buffer-name))
         (file (buffer-file-name))
         (lines (count-lines (point-min) (point-max)))
         (chars (- (point-max) (point-min)))
         (mode major-mode))
    (message (concat
              "Buffer: %s\n"
              "File: %s\n"
              "Mode: %s\n"
              "Lines: %d\n"
              "Characters: %d")
             name
             (or file "(not visiting a file)")
             mode
             lines
             chars)))

(global-set-key (kbd "C-c i") 'my-buffer-summary)
(my-buffer-summary)
